does: This paper introduces SpeechJBB, a systematic audio jailbreak benchmark for evaluating safety alignment in large audio language models. The authors create a dataset of code-switched and multilingual harmful prompts in audio form and test them against multiple state-of-the-art LALMs. Beyond simple code-switching, they augment the attack surface by inserting phonologically plausible pseudo-words around safety-critical terms to simulate localized obfuscation. Their key empirical finding is that code-switched harmful audio achieves substantially higher jailbreak success rates compared to monolingual text-based attacks, with non-English utterances and mixed-language pairs showing the highest vulnerability. The pseudo-word insertion technique further degrades model safety by reducing refusal rates, indicating that natural-sounding linguistic obfuscation can effectively circumvent existing safety mechanisms in audio models.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial robustness and safety alignment of large language models into the audio modality and multilingual domain. It builds on earlier jailbreak studies that focused on text-based harmful prompts and toxic content detection, while responding to the recognized gap that safety evaluation has lagged behind multimodal and multilingual deployment of generative systems. The paper connects to work on code-switching effects in NLP, cross-lingual transfer vulnerabilities, and acoustic adversarial examples. It also relates to broader literature on robustness evaluation frameworks and prompt injection attacks, adapting these methodologies to the unique challenges of spoken audio where pronunciation and phonetic properties create new attack vectors that text-only evaluations miss.
enables: This benchmark work establishes a foundation for developing more robust safety mechanisms in audio language models. It points toward future research on audio-specific alignment techniques that account for multilingual and code-switched inputs, as well as acoustic perturbations that text-based safety training cannot address. The findings suggest that safety teams need to explicitly include diverse linguistic modalities in their evaluation and training protocols. The work enables investigation into why audio models are more vulnerable to code-switching attacks than their text counterparts, potentially informing better safety architectures. It also opens directions for studying phonetic obfuscation as a systematic class of adversarial attacks and for designing defenses that remain robust across linguistic variations, pronunciation patterns, and acoustic mimicry strategies.
